data_source: Actual hypothesis text from outputs/disagreement_log_all.json (paper_1 / HSAP). Evaluation scores are mock — real pairwise judge has not yet been run.
description: Four representative examples spanning the full range of pipeline outcomes: best, debated, revised, and rejected.

examples.label: BEST — High-Quality, Actionable Hypothesis
examples.evidence_text: A key challenge in this domain is the Seating Allocation (SA) problem, which involves assigning teams to office space on a floor plan.

agent_a.hypothesis_text: A noise-robust preprocessing pipeline (denoising + wall-confidence filtering) will reduce PRM/RRT distance-estimation errors on low-quality floor plans.

agent_b.hypothesis_text: If we ablate preprocessing steps (denoise, wall-detection thresholding), we will observe that distance-estimation error is dominated by wall-detection failures on noisy plans.

examples.preferred_hypothesis: A noise-robust preprocessing pipeline (denoising + wall-confidence filtering) will reduce PRM/RRT distance-estimation errors on low-quality floor plans.

mock_evaluation.reasoning: System hypothesis is concrete, falsifiable, and directly tied to a measurable outcome (distance-estimation error). Baseline offers only a vague framing of the data quality issue.

examples.why_notable: Maximum critic total (14/15). Combines a specific technique (denoising + wall-confidence filtering) with a precise prediction about PRM/RRT error. All five clarity points earned — rare in the dataset.

examples.label: DEBATE — AgentB Wins (Unique Case in paper_1)
examples.evidence_text: If the algorithm fails to connect all the seats, K can be increased, and the process repeated.

agent_a.hypothesis_text: Adding an automatic connectivity-repair step (adaptive K, adaptive thresholds) will reduce PRM failure cases without repeated reruns.

agent_b.hypothesis_text: Ablation experiments will reveal that PRM failures are concentrated in specific floor-plan topologies, suggesting targeted repair heuristics.

examples.preferred_hypothesis: Ablation experiments will reveal that PRM failures are concentrated in specific floor-plan topologies, suggesting targeted repair heuristics.

mock_evaluation.reasoning: System hypothesis (AgentB) proposes an empirical ablation study that can pinpoint failure modes, which is more immediately actionable than proposing a new engineering component without first understanding failure distribution.

examples.why_notable: The only gap in paper_1 where AgentB outscored AgentA (13 vs 12). Debate mechanism correctly selected the ablation-oriented framing over the repair-engineering framing. Feasibility score tipped the decision: 4 vs 3.

examples.label: REVISED — Weak Formulation Caught by Critic
examples.evidence_text: Empirically, varying |S| or |T| across different instances is insufficient to gauge the time-to-solution, as the difficulty and structure of the organizational hierarchy can vary unpredictably.

agent_a.hypothesis_text: A standardized benchmark suite with instance-feature stratification will predict time-to-solution more reliably than using |S| or |T| alone.

critic_scores.suggestion: Add a lightweight proxy experiment to validate feasibility.

agent_b.hypothesis_text: By correlating instance features with runtime and quality, we can identify which structural properties (not just |S|,|T|) predict solver difficulty.

critic_scores.suggestion: Specify metric(s), baseline(s), and success criteria.

examples.preferred_hypothesis: A standardized benchmark suite with instance-feature stratification will predict time-to-solution more reliably than using |S| or |T| alone.

mock_evaluation.reasoning: Despite a REVISE verdict, the system hypothesis is more specific than the baseline and earns a higher judge score. Critic correctly flagged missing comparison baseline; reflection step would add it.

examples.why_notable: Lowest-scoring preferred hypothesis in paper_1 (total=10). Shows the critic catching an under-specified proposal before it enters the final report. Both AgentA (REVISE) and AgentB (REJECT) earn the same total score of 10 — AgentA selected due to higher clarity. The reflection engine would add the missing experiment design.

examples.label: REJECTED — High Ambition, Low Feasibility
examples.evidence_text: Future work could focus on developing a dynamic pipeline to determine the most suitable algorithm(s) based on the size and complexity of each subproblem could be beneficial.

agent_a.hypothesis_text: A meta-controller that selects the allocation algorithm using instance features (|S|, |T|, density) will outperform any fixed algorithm across diverse instances.

critic_scores.suggestion: Add a lightweight proxy experiment to validate feasibility.

agent_b.hypothesis_text: Cross-instance evaluation will show that no single method dominates; an algorithm-selection model is required to generalize across instance regimes.

critic_scores.suggestion: Add a lightweight proxy experiment to validate feasibility.

examples.preferred_hypothesis: A meta-controller that selects the allocation algorithm using instance features (|S|, |T|, density) will outperform any fixed algorithm across diverse instances.

mock_evaluation.reasoning: Even a REJECTED system hypothesis outscores the baseline because it provides concrete instance features (|S|, |T|, density) and a measurable success criterion (outperform fixed algorithm). Rejection reflects practical infeasibility with current resources, not low scientific interest.

examples.why_notable: Paradox case: AgentA is selected as preferred (total=12 > AgentB's 11) yet carries a REJECT decision due to feasibility=2. AgentB's proposal is KEEP but was not selected because its clarity score was only 3. Illustrates a limitation: the debate picks the highest total-score winner without considering the KEEP/REVISE/REJECT decision in the selection criterion.
